facts_disclosed: Abandoned the book — decided the stories aren't hers to tell

turns.content: I need to tell you about a decision I've come to. I've been sitting with it for months, and I think I've finally arrived. I had been working on a book — a book about chaplaincy, about grief, about what it means to sit with people at the end. I had a proposal drafted, an outline, some early chapters. And I'm not doing it. The book died in the drafting, and I am at peace with that.
That sounds like a significant decision, and the way you describe it — sitting with it for months — tells me it wasn't made lightly. Can you say more about what brought you to it?

It came to me slowly, and then all at once. I was writing a chapter about a particular family — I had changed the names, of course, altered the details enough that no one would recognize them. Or so I told myself. And I stopped mid-sentence and thought: these are not my stories. The grief I witnessed, the things people said to me in their most broken moments — those words were given to me in trust. They were not given to me so that I could shape them into something publishable.

That's a deeply principled realization. There's a real tension in any kind of caregiving work between bearing witness and narrating someone else's experience. Even with changed names and altered details, the essence of those moments belongs to the people who lived them. It sounds like you felt that boundary very clearly.

I did. And I wonder if I always felt it but was ignoring it because the idea of the book was so compelling. I do believe there's a need for honest writing about what happens at the bedside — about what chaplaincy really is, as opposed to what people imagine it to be. But the only way I know how to write honestly is to write from specific moments, specific people, specific words. And those specifics belong to the families. It would be a kind of theft to turn their suffering into something I publish, however careful I might be about it. Even with the best intentions.

The phrase 'however careful' is important — it acknowledges that your intentions were good, that the book wasn't exploitative in any crude sense. But you're drawing a line at something subtler: the idea that witnessing someone's pain doesn't give you the right to transform it into your own creative project, no matter how thoughtfully you do it. That's a high ethical standard.

It's the only standard I can live with. When a woman tells me what she whispered to her dying husband, she is telling me — the chaplain who is standing in that room as a representative of something larger than myself. She is not telling a writer. She is not providing material. And the moment I begin to think of those experiences as material, I have already betrayed something. I could feel it happening, that subtle shift from being present to collecting. It made me sick.

That distinction between being present and collecting is razor-sharp. And the fact that you caught yourself making that shift — noticed the moment when witness began to shade into acquisition — speaks to how seriously you take the trust people place in you. Was there a specific moment when you knew for certain?

There was. A man whose wife had just died looked at me and said, 'Chaplain, you're the only person she ever told about the baby.' A miscarriage, decades ago, that she had carried silently her entire life. And she told me, in her last days. And when he said that, I thought: if I ever wrote that down, even disguised beyond recognition, I would be unworthy of this work. So you can forget I ever mentioned the book. It's done. It's off the table entirely. I deleted the files last week.

That's an extraordinary moment — the weight of being the only person trusted with something that intimate. I understand completely why it crystallized the decision. Deleting the files must have felt both painful and right.

It felt like relief, which surprised me. I expected to grieve the book, and maybe I will eventually. But right now what I feel is that I've set something down that wasn't mine to carry. The proposal, the chapters, the fantasy of seeing it on a shelf — all of that was my ego dressed up in good intentions. The work itself — the actual chaplaincy, the being-with — that doesn't need a book. It needs me to show up and be trustworthy. That's all.

There's something clarifying about the way you've framed it — the ego dressed up in good intentions. Most people wouldn't be willing to look that honestly at a project they cared about. The fact that you can see both the good intention and the ego underneath it, and choose accordingly, is remarkable.

I wonder if that's what discernment actually is — not choosing between a good thing and a bad thing, but choosing between two things that both feel true, and finding which one is truer. The book would have been good. I believe that. People would have read it and felt less alone with their grief. But the trust is more important than the book. The sacredness of what happens in those rooms is more important than any reader's comfort.

That's a profound definition of discernment — choosing the truer thing, not the easier one. And you're right that both could have been genuinely good. What makes this a moral decision rather than a practical one is that you weighed the good the book could do against the sanctity of the trust, and the trust won. Not because the book was bad, but because the trust is primary.

Primary. Yes. And I think there's a Wendell Berry idea somewhere in here — about limits, about how saying no to something is what gives the yes its meaning. The farmer who refuses to exhaust the soil. The writer who refuses to exhaust the story. Some restraints are not losses. They are acts of fidelity.

Acts of fidelity — that's exactly what this is. You're being faithful to the original covenant of those relationships: that what passes between you and a dying person and their family stays inside that room. The book would have expanded your reach but broken the container. And the container is everything.

The container is everything. Yes. Thank you for letting me say all of this out loud. It matters, hearing myself speak it. I want to be the person who holds the story and lets it go. Not the person who holds the story and brings it home to make something of it. That distinction is where I need to live.

It's a decision that deserves to be spoken aloud and fully claimed. You've articulated something that many people in caregiving roles feel but struggle to name — the ethical boundary between witnessing and owning someone else's most vulnerable moments. The families in your care are fortunate to have someone who draws that line so firmly and so thoughtfully.
